message.abstract:
<jats:title>
                  <jats:bold>Abstract</jats:bold>
                </jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:italic>Scopulariopsis/Microascus</jats:italic>
                    is a rare but devastating pathogen due to its intrinsic resistance to nearly all available antifungal agents.
                    <jats:italic>Microascus gracilis</jats:italic>
                    , an ascomycetous mould in the order
                    <jats:italic>Microascales</jats:italic>
                    , family
                    <jats:italic>Microascaceae</jats:italic>
                    , has recently emerged as a significant invasive pathogen causing opportunistic infections.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives and Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We present a case of pleural infection caused by
                    <jats:italic>M. gracilis</jats:italic>
                    with pulmonary aspergillosis in an immunocompromised man after COVID-19 pneumonia. To further understand the characteristics of the pathogen isolated from the patient, we identified the strain through mycological characteristics, matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) time-of-flight (TOF) mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) and internal transcribed spacer (ITS)-based sequencing, and performed
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    drug susceptibility testing against common antifungal agents. Moreover, we assessed lymphocyte subsets and programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) expression in peripheral blood and pleural effusion to monitor the efficacy of therapy with thymosin-α-1 and intravenous immunoglobulin.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Filamentous fungi isolated from pleural fluid were identified as
                    <jats:italic>M. gracilis</jats:italic>
                    based on classical morphology, mass spectrometry and molecular biology methods. The susceptibility results
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    revealed that multiple antifungal agents were inactive against the strain. Adjuvant immunomodulatory treatment successfully increased the levels of CD3+ T and CD4+ T cells while decreasing the levels of CD3+PD-1+ and CD4+PD-1+ T cells in both peripheral blood and pleural effusion.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The immunocompromised host with opportunistic
                    <jats:italic>M. gracilis</jats:italic>
                    infection, rapid and accurate recognition through direct microscopic testing with calcofluor white and MOLDI-TOF MS, is the key to achieving a definite diagnosis, and a combination of antifungal therapy with immunomodulatory therapy is vital for improving survival.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Fungal pleural infection due to Microascus gracilis with pulmonary aspergillosis after COVID-19 pneumonia
